project: Exo Agent Runtime and Provider Expansion
overview: Expand Exo's built-in provider surface, harden Vertex AI reliability, add configurable agent-runtime controls, stage infinite-context capabilities behind research-first decision memos, add HITL and tool-result metadata support, and close Temporal parity gaps across the Python runtime, distributed execution, web APIs/UI, docs, and examples.
goals: Add built-in providers for AzureOpenAI, VertexOpenAI, Ollama, LMStudio, and LiteLLM using a shared OpenAI-compatible foundation where the wire protocol matches.
Harden the existing Vertex AI provider with transient retry backoff and credential/session recovery for long-running agents.

Add agent-definition controls for planner delegation, budget awareness, hidden tool-argument injection, parallel sub-agents, spawn_self additional context, HITL tool gating, and MCP progress streaming toggles.
Allow sub-agent creation flows to specify child tool subsets and structured-output schemas.
Attach explicit metadata to tool call results and tool-result events, including execution timing and approval state.
Turn current context-management behavior into a staged, research-backed infinite-context program with persisted summaries, checkpoints, retrieval-aware compaction, and stronger regression coverage.
Make Temporal execution match local execution for streaming, tools, progress events, planning, context, memory, and advanced agent controls.
Expose all required new configuration across Exo Web storage, APIs, editor UI, docs, and examples.
nonGoals: Do not implement a single giant refactor that exceeds the story-size budget.
Do not introduce duplicate provider implementations when a shared OpenAI-compatible base is sufficient.
Do not claim unlimited or perfect infinite context; ship staged capabilities with explicit limits and regression coverage.
Do not silently diverge Temporal behavior from local behavior for advanced runtime features.
Do not add user-visible tool parameters to actual tool execution signatures when the requirement is LLM-visible schema injection only.
successMetrics: Each new provider resolves through the registry, passes focused provider tests, and has a documented configuration path.
Vertex runs recover from transient failures and credential-expiry scenarios with deterministic success or deterministic operator-facing errors.
Serialized agent configs round-trip across Agent.to_dict(), Agent.from_dict(), distributed worker payloads, and Temporal workflows with the new fields intact.
Planner-enabled agents route through a planner first, but planner messages never enter executor history.
HITL-marked tools pause for approval, execute only after approval, and return deterministic rejection or timeout results when approval is denied or missing.
Tool call results expose structured metadata such as execution time, start and completion timestamps, and approval state across runtime and streaming surfaces.
Temporal runs emit the same externally observable detailed events as local runs for text, tool calls, tool results, MCP progress, and cancellation boundaries.
Context growth remains bounded in long-running conversations while branch isolation and retrieval relevance remain intact.

stack.framework: Python 3.11 UV workspace monorepo with FastAPI backend services, Astro frontend pages, and TypeScript islands where needed.
stack.hosting: Existing Exo deployment targets; no hosting-platform change is part of this PRD.
stack.database: SQLite-backed Exo Web metadata plus Redis-backed distributed execution state and event transport.
stack.auth: Existing Exo Web session-cookie authentication with role-based route guards.

routes.name: Agent List
routes.purpose: Entry point for creating and managing agents whose new runtime flags must remain discoverable.

routes.name: Agent Editor
routes.purpose: Primary UI surface for planning, budget, tool-injection, progress, and parallel-subagent settings.

routes.name: Playground
routes.purpose: Interactive validation surface for testing new providers and runtime behavior.

routes.name: Agent CRUD API
routes.purpose: Persists agent-definition fields that must round-trip to live Agent instances.

routes.name: Provider CRUD API
routes.purpose: Stores provider type, endpoint, retry, and auth configuration for the expanded provider set.

routes.name: Provider Connectivity Test API
routes.purpose: Validates provider connectivity and auth expectations for local and cloud providers.

routes.name: Run Event Stream
routes.purpose: Carries run-progress updates, including MCP progress, into the web layer.

routes.name: Agents Guide
routes.purpose: Documents new agent-definition fields and runtime behavior.

routes.name: API Reference
routes.purpose: Documents the updated provider and agent API contracts.

uiNotes: Provider-selection surfaces must list the new provider types without breaking the existing provider and model-selection flow.
Agent editor validation must reject malformed budget strings and parallel-subagent counts above 7 before save.
The existing approvals experience should be reused or extended for HITL tool approvals instead of introducing a separate human-approval UX.
Hidden tool-argument injection is configurable in the agent editor but is never rendered as a real tool argument on execution logs or downstream tool calls.
Tool-result inspection surfaces should display execution metadata such as execution time and approval state when available.
Planner-specific messages must remain invisible in the normal conversation history and execution transcript shown to end users.
Run-stream surfaces must preserve current behavior by default and only suppress MCP progress events when the new agent flag disables them.
Public docs and examples must show at least one cloud provider and one local/OpenAI-compatible provider.

importFormat.description: Serialized provider, agent-definition, and sub-agent request shapes that must round-trip across runtime, distributed, and web surfaces.

injected_tool_args.ui_request_id: Opaque UI correlation id visible only to the LLM schema.

rules: Every implementation story must depend on a completed research memo for its subsystem before code changes begin.
Each implementation story must stay within roughly 150 to 200 changed lines; if the slice grows beyond that, split it again before work starts.
Public behavior changes require docstrings, reference docs, a user-facing guide/example, and at least one negative-case test.
Shared OpenAI-compatible providers must reuse the common base unless the research memo explicitly marks a wire-level incompatibility.
Planner execution uses the same tools as the executor agent, but planner messages and planner tool chatter never enter the executor's persisted message history.

Budget-awareness accepts only two forms: per-message or limit:<0-100>; limit mode means the agent must compact context before a new model call and stop with a deterministic budget-limit message if usage still exceeds the configured percentage.
When budget-awareness is per-message, every assistant message and tool result surfaced back to the model must include a context-window advisory string with current and optimal token counts.

Hidden tool-argument injection adds schema-only fields to all tool definitions presented to the LLM, captures returned values in tool-call metadata, and strips them from the actual tool kwargs before execution.

Sub-agent creation through spawn_self or the parallel sub-agent tool may optionally specify a child tool subset and a JSON-schema output contract; child results must be validated against that schema before aggregation.

Parallel sub-agent execution is opt-in, capped at 7 child jobs per call, accepts a custom system prompt and explicit parent-tool subset for each child, and returns a structured aggregated result with per-job success or error entries.

spawn_self gains an additional_context parameter plus optional tool_names and output_schema child overrides that are passed only to the child execution context and do not mutate the parent agent definition.

hitl_tools is a list of existing tool names on the agent; matching tool calls must create a human approval request before execution and return a deterministic rejected or timed_out tool result when approval is denied or missing.
emit_mcp_progress defaults to true for backward compatibility; false suppresses MCP progress events in streams without changing tool execution semantics.
Tool-result objects and tool-result events must carry metadata including status, started_at, completed_at, execution_time_ms, and approval state when applicable.

Stage 1 context compaction must assemble prompts using the latest persisted summary or checkpoint, at most the 2 most recent raw turns, up to the top 3 retrieved relevant summaries, and the current user turn.
Context summaries and checkpoints are branch-scoped; child branches may read inherited parent checkpoints but must never write back into parent or sibling context state.
Temporal execution must preserve the same externally observable detailed-event ordering as local execution for text, tool calls, tool results, progress notifications, and cancellation.
Compatibility gaps found during verification must become either failing tests to fix immediately or explicit skipped tests with a written reason; silent omissions are not allowed.
qualityGates: uv run ruff check <affected package paths>

stories.title: Research provider and Vertex contracts
stories.description: As an Exo maintainer, I want a provider and Vertex decision memo so that implementation stories reuse the right abstractions and do not guess about auth, endpoint, or retry behavior.
acceptanceCriteria: Produce a written decision memo that inventories the current openai and vertex providers, the provider registry, provider CRUD/testing routes, and the exact gaps for AzureOpenAI, VertexOpenAI, Ollama, LMStudio, and LiteLLM.
The memo must choose the shared OpenAI-compatible base strategy and explicitly list which fields are common versus provider-specific.
Example: include one concrete mapping for azureopenai and one for vertexopenai from stored provider config to the final Exo model string and request configuration.
Negative case: list at least one provider behavior that must not be forced into the shared base because it would break auth, endpoint resolution, or model naming.

stories.title: Research agent-runtime control contracts
stories.description: As an Exo maintainer, I want an agent-runtime design memo so that planning, budget, tool-injection, sub-agent, HITL, and progress controls can be implemented without interface churn.
acceptanceCriteria: Produce a decision memo covering planning_enabled, planning_model, planning_instructions, budget_awareness, hitl_tools, emit_mcp_progress, injected_tool_args, allow_parallel_subagents, max_parallel_subagents, child tool subsets, child output_schema contracts, and tool-result metadata.
The memo must identify the exact touchpoints in Agent, runner, serialization, distributed worker payloads, and Exo Web agent storage/runtime.
Example: include one serialized agent-config JSON example showing all new fields with their default and non-default behaviors.
Negative case: explicitly reject planner-history mixing, raw tool-kwarg pollution from injected fields, parallel-subagent counts above 7, and HITL tool names that are not present on the agent.

stories.title: Research context-management and infinite-context stages
stories.description: As an Exo maintainer, I want a context-management research memo so that infinite-context work is staged from evidence instead of guesswork.
acceptanceCriteria: Produce a decision memo that inventories current summarization, trimming, vector injection, branch isolation, memory integration, and workspace/artifact behavior in Exo.
The memo must summarize external research and vendor/system approaches relevant to long-context and context-compaction workflows, then select a staged implementation path for Exo.
Example: include one long-running conversation flow that shows when summaries, checkpoints, retrieval, and compaction should happen.
Negative case: explicitly list at least one over-scoped infinite-context behavior that is out of scope for this PRD.

stories.title: Research Temporal parity gaps
stories.description: As an Exo maintainer, I want a Temporal parity memo so that durable execution is brought to feature parity with local execution in small, testable slices.
acceptanceCriteria: Produce a decision memo that compares the current local execution path against the current Temporal path for streaming, tool execution, MCP progress, memory, context, planning, and cancellation.
The memo must define the required externally observable parity contract and the order in which missing behavior will be closed.
Example: include one expected detailed-event trace for the same task under local and Temporal execution.
Negative case: identify at least one current Temporal limitation that must fail explicitly rather than silently degrading behavior.

stories.title: Add shared OpenAI-compatible provider base
stories.description: As a provider maintainer, I want a shared OpenAI-compatible provider base so that compatible providers can be added with thin adapters instead of duplicated transport code.
acceptanceCriteria: Add a reusable provider layer that centralizes OpenAI-compatible message conversion, tool conversion, streaming chunk parsing, finish-reason mapping, base_url handling, and provider-specific auth/header hooks.
Existing openai behavior must remain unchanged from a public API perspective while reusing shared helpers where safe.
Example: a thin provider can override only endpoint/auth settings and still execute complete and stream through the shared path.
Negative case: providers with non-OpenAI wire semantics are not forced into this base and continue to use their dedicated implementation path.

stories.title: Add AzureOpenAI provider
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want a built-in AzureOpenAI provider so that Azure-hosted deployments can run without custom provider glue.
acceptanceCriteria: Register an azureopenai provider that uses the shared OpenAI-compatible base and the Azure deployment-oriented endpoint/auth contract chosen in the research memo.
The provider must accept a deployment-oriented model string and any provider-specific endpoint metadata needed to resolve requests correctly.
Example: get_provider resolves an azureopenai deployment string and produces a successful request configuration against an Azure endpoint.
Negative case: missing Azure endpoint or required deployment metadata returns a deterministic configuration error before any network request is attempted.

stories.title: Add VertexOpenAI provider
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want a dedicated VertexOpenAI provider so that Vertex benchmark or OpenAI-compatible endpoints are separate from the Gemini SDK-based Vertex provider.
acceptanceCriteria: Register a vertexopenai provider that uses the shared OpenAI-compatible base and the provider-specific endpoint/auth fields chosen in the research memo.
The provider must remain a separate code path from the existing vertex provider and must not route through the Google Gemini SDK implementation.
Example: a vertexopenai model string resolves to the OpenAI-compatible request path while preserving Vertex-specific endpoint settings.
Negative case: attempts to use vertexopenai without the required endpoint metadata fail clearly instead of silently falling back to the existing vertex provider.

stories.title: Add Ollama provider via shared base
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want a first-class Ollama provider so that local models can be configured as a built-in option instead of a custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint workaround.
acceptanceCriteria: Register an ollama provider that uses the shared OpenAI-compatible base with the local-default endpoint rules documented in the provider memo.
The provider must work with a local default base_url while still allowing overrides through provider config.
Example: an ollama provider resolves to the local server endpoint and executes a request without requiring a cloud API key.
Negative case: an unreachable local server returns a bounded, actionable error rather than a raw traceback or cloud-provider fallback.

stories.title: Add LMStudio provider via shared base
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want a built-in LMStudio provider so that local OpenAI-compatible serving can be selected directly in Exo.
acceptanceCriteria: Register an lmstudio provider that uses the shared OpenAI-compatible base and the local-default endpoint rules selected in the provider memo.
The provider must work without cloud-specific auth requirements when the local server is configured without an API key.
Example: get_provider resolves lmstudio to the configured local endpoint and uses the shared message and streaming logic.
Negative case: missing or unreachable LMStudio base_url returns a deterministic provider-connection error instead of attempting OpenAI defaults.

stories.title: Add LiteLLM provider via shared base
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want a built-in LiteLLM provider so that LiteLLM proxies can be configured without treating them as an unspecified custom provider.
acceptanceCriteria: Register a litellm provider that uses the shared OpenAI-compatible base and requires an explicit proxy base_url.
The provider must support API-key-based auth when configured through provider storage or direct model config.
Example: a litellm model string resolves through get_provider and builds requests against a configured proxy endpoint.
Negative case: litellm without a base_url must fail validation immediately and must not silently fall back to the default OpenAI endpoint.

stories.title: Expand web provider enums and model resolution
stories.description: As an Exo Web maintainer, I want the backend provider surfaces to understand the expanded provider set so that saved providers and agent runtime resolution remain consistent.
acceptanceCriteria: Update provider-type validation, provider runtime resolution, and any model-selection helpers so the expanded provider set is accepted and resolved correctly from stored config.
Existing openai, anthropic, gemini, vertex, ollama, and custom behavior must continue to work unchanged.
Example: creating each new provider type through the API yields stored config that AgentService can resolve into a live ModelProvider.
Negative case: unknown provider_type values still return HTTP 422 instead of being accepted as generic custom providers.

stories.title: Add provider connectivity tests for new providers
stories.description: As an Exo Web maintainer, I want provider test endpoints to understand the new provider types so that users can validate configuration before running agents.
acceptanceCriteria: Extend provider connectivity-test logic with the endpoint and auth expectations for azureopenai, vertexopenai, lmstudio, and litellm while preserving the existing ollama path.
The test path must return structured success, error, and latency values without leaking secrets.
Example: POST /api/v1/providers/:id/test succeeds against a stubbed OpenAI-compatible provider for each new built-in type.
Negative case: missing required base_url or an unreachable local server returns a bounded error string instead of an unhandled exception.

stories.title: Document provider additions
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want docs and examples for the new providers so that I can configure them without reading source code.
acceptanceCriteria: Update provider reference docs, model guides, and examples with the new provider strings, config requirements, and expected local-versus-cloud differences.
The documentation must include at least one cloud OpenAI-compatible provider and one local provider example.
Example: a quickstart or reference example shows a working ollama or lmstudio configuration plus one AzureOpenAI or VertexOpenAI configuration.
Negative case: the docs must not claim unsupported auth, multimodal, or endpoint parity for a provider where that support is not implemented.

stories.title: Add Vertex transient retry backoff
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want the Vertex provider to retry transient failures with exponential backoff so that short-lived service failures do not crash agent runs.
acceptanceCriteria: Add exponential backoff to the existing vertex provider for transient failures chosen in the research memo, honoring configured retry limits for both complete and stream paths.
Retry behavior must be implemented without introducing uncontrolled infinite loops or masking terminal errors.
Example: a simulated transient 429 or 5xx failure retries and then succeeds within the configured retry budget.
Negative case: invalid credentials, malformed config, or terminal client errors do not enter the backoff loop and fail deterministically.

stories.title: Recover Vertex credentials and sessions
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want the Vertex provider to recover when credentials expire or change mid-run so that long-running agents do not fail on stale auth state.
acceptanceCriteria: Update Vertex credential and client lifecycle handling so expired or replaced credentials trigger refresh or client rehydration rather than stale-client reuse.
The fix must support both ADC-style auth and service-account-derived credentials where the current provider supports them.
Example: a run that starts with a valid credential source and hits an expiry path rebuilds the client and continues successfully.
Negative case: malformed service-account content or a missing credential source still fails with a deterministic operator-facing error.

stories.title: Cover Vertex reliability with tests and docs
stories.description: As an Exo maintainer, I want explicit tests and operator docs for Vertex reliability behavior so that retries and auth recovery remain stable over time.
acceptanceCriteria: Add focused provider tests for transient backoff, credential refresh, and failure classification, plus operator docs that describe the supported auth modes and failure handling.
The tests must cover both complete and stream paths where the behavior differs.
Example: the docs include one supported ADC workflow and one service-account workflow with the expected recovery behavior.
Negative case: the docs explicitly warn that stale credentials are rebuilt or rejected, not silently reused forever.

stories.title: Extend serializable agent-definition schema
stories.description: As an Exo maintainer, I want the new runtime controls to be first-class serializable agent fields so that local, distributed, and web-created agents share one contract.
acceptanceCriteria: Extend Agent construction and serialization contracts with planning_enabled, planning_model, planning_instructions, budget_awareness, hitl_tools, emit_mcp_progress, injected_tool_args, allow_parallel_subagents, and max_parallel_subagents.
Defaults must preserve current behavior: planning disabled, emit_mcp_progress true, no injected tool args, and no parallel sub-agent tool unless explicitly enabled.
Example: an agent with all new fields round-trips through Agent.to_dict() and Agent.from_dict() without losing values.
Negative case: malformed budget strings, hitl_tools entries that do not match registered tools, or max_parallel_subagents values above 7 are rejected at construction or deserialization time.

stories.title: Route enabled agents through a planner
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want planner-enabled agents to produce a plan before execution so that complex tasks can be decomposed consistently.
acceptanceCriteria: When planning_enabled is true, the run path must first invoke a planner agent that receives the same tool set as the executor agent and uses planning_model and planning_instructions when provided, otherwise defaulting to the executor values.
After planning completes, the executor agent must receive the planner output plus the original task as its input context.
Example: a planner-enabled agent produces a short plan, then the executor uses that plan to perform the task.
Negative case: an agent with planning disabled bypasses the planner completely and preserves current execution behavior.

stories.title: Isolate planner history from executor history
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want planner messages hidden from executor history so that planning remains an internal control step rather than user-visible conversation state.
acceptanceCriteria: Planner messages, planner tool chatter, and planner-only intermediate state must not be appended to the executor agent's message history or persisted conversational memory.
Distributed worker and serialized execution paths must preserve this same planner-history isolation.
Example: after a planner-enabled run, the persisted executor history contains the original user task and the executor interaction, but not planner messages.
Negative case: a planner failure must not poison executor history with partial planner content.

stories.title: Implement budget-awareness modes
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want agent-level budget-awareness modes so that runs react to context pressure before quality collapses.
acceptanceCriteria: Implement per-message and limit:<0-100> budget-awareness modes according to the design memo and the PRD rules.
Per-message mode must append a context-window advisory string using current and optimal token counts before the next model decision can be made.

Example: an agent with budget_awareness set to per-message emits the advisory after assistant output and tool results, and an agent with limit:70 compacts before the next model call when estimated context usage reaches 70 percent.
Negative case: malformed limit values such as limit:abc or limit:101 are rejected before execution.

stories.title: Inject hidden tool arguments into schemas only
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want to add LLM-visible tool arguments globally from the agent definition so that UI or routing metadata can be captured without changing real tool signatures.
acceptanceCriteria: Add an injected_tool_args agent field whose entries are merged into every tool schema presented to the LLM while remaining absent from the actual tool function signature and kwargs.
Returned values for injected fields must be captured in tool-call metadata for observers while being stripped before the real tool execution call is made.
Example: the LLM can return a ui_request_id for any tool call and the value is available in metadata without breaking the underlying tool execution.
Negative case: if a real tool already defines the same parameter name, the agent must reject the conflicting injected field rather than silently shadowing it.

stories.title: Add child overrides to spawn_self
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want spawn_self to accept child overrides so that child runs can receive targeted task framing, tool subsets, and structured-output contracts without mutating the parent agent.
acceptanceCriteria: Extend spawn_self to accept additional_context plus optional tool_names and output_schema arguments that are passed only into the child execution context and do not modify the parent instructions or persisted parent history.
Child execution must keep the same current isolation rules for memory and context branching unless the existing runtime already documents otherwise.
Example: spawn_self is called with a task, additional context, a restricted tool subset, and an output_schema that validates the child result before it is returned to the parent.
Negative case: empty overrides preserve the current spawn_self behavior exactly, and unknown tool names or invalid output_schema values are rejected before the child starts.

stories.title: Register the parallel sub-agent tool contract
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want a built-in parallel sub-agent tool contract so that agents can request bounded parallel work with explicit child prompts, tool subsets, and structured-output schemas.
acceptanceCriteria: Add allow_parallel_subagents and max_parallel_subagents controls plus a built-in tool schema that accepts up to 7 child jobs, each with task, system_prompt, tool_names, and output_schema fields.
The tool must only be registered when allow_parallel_subagents is enabled and must validate tool names against the parent agent's registered tools.
Example: an enabled agent exposes a tool that accepts two child jobs with different system prompts, different allowed tool subsets, and different structured-output contracts.
Negative case: a call with more than 7 jobs, with an unknown tool name, or with an invalid output_schema is rejected before any child agent starts.

stories.title: Execute parallel sub-agents with bounded aggregation
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want the parallel sub-agent tool to execute child jobs concurrently and return structured aggregated results so that complex work can be decomposed safely.
acceptanceCriteria: Parallel sub-agent execution must run child jobs concurrently, pass only the requested parent-tool subset, apply each child's custom system prompt, validate each child output against its optional output_schema, and preserve the same memory and context isolation rules used for spawned children.
The tool must return a structured aggregate result with one entry per child job containing success output or a bounded error value plus any validated structured payload.
Example: two child jobs run in parallel and the aggregate result contains one validated structured output object and one plain-text output keyed by job index or child identifier.

Negative case: if one child fails, unrelated child jobs continue and the aggregate result records the failed child without cancelling the entire call unless all children fail before useful work begins.

stories.title: Add MCP progress streaming toggle
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want MCP progress updates to be toggleable per agent so that run streams can be noisy or quiet depending on the consuming client.
acceptanceCriteria: Add an emit_mcp_progress agent flag that defaults to true and gates whether MCP progress notifications are surfaced through local and web stream outputs.
Tool execution semantics must remain unchanged regardless of the progress-streaming setting.
Example: an agent with emit_mcp_progress enabled continues to emit MCPProgressEvent items before the tool result event.
Negative case: an agent with emit_mcp_progress disabled emits no MCP progress events but still emits the final tool result and final completion events.

stories.title: Strengthen progress streaming regression coverage
stories.description: As an Exo maintainer, I want rigorous progress-streaming tests so that MCP progress behavior does not silently regress in direct or web-backed execution.
acceptanceCriteria: Add focused regression coverage for direct run.stream behavior, MCP stdio tools, and web-backed run streaming where progress events must appear in the correct order.
The tests must cover both the enabled and disabled emit_mcp_progress settings.
Example: a streaming test asserts that at least one MCP progress event appears before the corresponding tool result when the flag is enabled.
Negative case: a paired test asserts that the same run emits no MCP progress events when the flag is disabled.

stories.title: Persist summary and checkpoint foundations
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want context summaries and checkpoints to be persisted instead of remaining transient so that long-running context can survive compaction and branch execution.
acceptanceCriteria: Implement the stage-1 context foundation chosen in the research memo by persisting summaries or checkpoints in a way the next run can load without replaying the full raw history.
The foundation must preserve current branch isolation guarantees and work with existing memory/context abstractions.
Example: after a summary-triggering run, the next run can load a persisted summary or checkpoint artifact instead of depending only on transient system-message injection.
Negative case: when the conversation remains below the summary threshold, no unnecessary persisted summary artifact is written.

stories.title: Assemble compacted context with retrieval stage 1
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want compacted context assembly to rehydrate only the most useful prior information so that long runs remain both bounded and relevant.
acceptanceCriteria: Implement the stage-1 compaction rule from this PRD: system prompt plus latest persisted summary or checkpoint, at most 2 most recent raw turns, up to the top 3 retrieved relevant summaries, and the current user turn.
Context assembly must use deterministic ordering so regression tests can assert what was injected.
Example: a follow-up query after compaction receives the latest checkpoint plus only the most relevant retrieved summary artifacts.
Negative case: irrelevant or low-priority summary artifacts are not injected when the retrieval selection rules exclude them.

stories.title: Add branch-scoped checkpoint continuation
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want spawned or branched runs to continue from inherited checkpoints without leaking context state across branches.
acceptanceCriteria: Implement the stage-2 checkpoint continuation behavior chosen in the research memo so child branches can read inherited parent checkpoints while writing their own branch-scoped updates.
Child runs created by spawn_self or the parallel sub-agent tool must follow the same checkpoint inheritance and isolation contract.
Example: a child branch starts from the parent's latest checkpoint and produces its own branch-local follow-up checkpoint after further work.
Negative case: sibling branches must never read each other's newly written checkpoints.

stories.title: Add long-run context regression coverage
stories.description: As an Exo maintainer, I want stronger context-management tests so that summarization, trimming, retrieval, branching, and budget limits are all proven to work together.
acceptanceCriteria: Add focused integration or marathon coverage for persisted summaries, compacted context assembly, branch checkpoint isolation, and budget-aware stopping behavior.
The tests must verify both bounded context growth and continued answer quality across multiple turns.
Example: a long conversation triggers summaries, retrieval, and checkpoint continuation while still producing a non-empty answer within the bounded context window.
Negative case: a regression test proves that branch-local context data does not leak into sibling or parent execution.

stories.title: Stream detailed Temporal events instead of final text only
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want Temporal execution to stream the same detailed events as local execution so that durable runs preserve observability and progress reporting.
acceptanceCriteria: Replace the current final-text-only Temporal execution bridge with a detailed event bridge that forwards text, tool, tool-result metadata, usage, and MCP progress events through the distributed publisher path.
The event ordering contract must match the local execution path for the same task when detailed streaming is enabled.
Example: a Temporal run publishes text deltas, tool-call events, MCP progress events, and final completion in the same observable order as a local run.
Negative case: if Temporal is unavailable or the bridge fails, the error must be explicit and must not silently degrade to a final-text-only success result.

stories.title: Support advanced agent configs in Temporal
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want planning, budget, HITL, tool-injection, and sub-agent features to behave the same under Temporal as they do locally.
acceptanceCriteria: Extend Temporal task reconstruction and execution so the new serializable agent fields behave the same under durable execution as under local execution.
The implementation must preserve the same planner-history isolation, hidden-tool-arg stripping, HITL approval gating, and child output-schema validation rules already required locally.

Example: a serialized agent with planning enabled, budget awareness, hitl_tools, hidden tool args, and parallel sub-agents produces the same externally visible behavior under local and Temporal execution.
Negative case: non-serializable runtime state still fails before task enqueue rather than being partially reconstructed inside Temporal.

stories.title: Support context and memory parity in Temporal
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want Temporal runs to preserve context compaction and memory behavior so that durable execution does not break long-context agent quality.
acceptanceCriteria: Temporal execution must preserve persisted summaries, checkpoints, retrieval-aware compaction, and long-running memory behavior according to the same rules used locally.
Temporal heartbeats and cancellation must remain compatible with long-running context-compacting runs.
Example: a long-running Temporal task survives activity heartbeats and continues from the expected compacted context state.
Negative case: cancellation stops further context mutations and prevents new events from being published after the cancellation boundary.

stories.title: Persist new agent fields through Exo Web APIs
stories.description: As an Exo Web user, I want the backend agent CRUD surfaces to store and return the new runtime fields so that web-created agents match Python-defined agents.
acceptanceCriteria: Add the required database, migration, API-schema, and runtime-bridge changes so planning, budget, hitl_tools, hidden tool args, progress toggle, and parallel-subagent flags round-trip through /api/v1/agents and AgentService.
Stored agent rows must continue to build a valid live Agent instance with the existing fields preserved.
Example: GET and PUT for an agent return the new fields and AgentService.build_agent reconstructs the same runtime settings.
Negative case: malformed JSON or invalid new-field values return HTTP 422 rather than partially saving broken agent state.

stories.title: Expose new agent controls in the web editor
stories.description: As an Exo Web user, I want the agent editor to expose the new runtime controls so that I can configure them without editing raw JSON.
acceptanceCriteria: Update the /agents/:id/edit experience with controls for planner enablement and overrides, budget awareness, hitl_tools, hidden tool args, MCP progress streaming, and parallel sub-agents.
The UI must surface validation and sensible defaults that match the runtime contract.
Example: a user can enable planning, set limit:70 budget awareness, mark deploy_service as a HITL tool, add a hidden tool arg definition, and enable parallel sub-agents with a max count of 4.
Negative case: the UI prevents saving malformed budget strings, duplicate hidden tool arg names, unknown HITL tool names, or max_parallel_subagents values above 7.

stories.title: Publish docs, examples, and migration notes
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want the new provider, agent, context, HITL, tool-metadata, and Temporal behavior documented end-to-end so that rollout does not depend on reading tests or source code.
acceptanceCriteria: Update reference docs, guides, API docs, getting-started material, examples, and migration notes for the new providers and agent-runtime controls, including HITL tools and tool-result metadata.
The documentation must include at least one complete agent-definition example and one Temporal-compatible execution example.
Example: docs show a planner-enabled agent with budget awareness, hitl_tools, child output_schema usage, and a local or cloud OpenAI-compatible provider configuration.
Negative case: any known unsupported combination remaining after implementation is called out explicitly instead of being implied to work.

stories.title: Run final compatibility verification
stories.description: As an Exo maintainer, I want a final compatibility verification pass so that the roadmap closes with explicit evidence rather than scattered story-level confidence.
acceptanceCriteria: Add or update a focused integration or marathon verification matrix that exercises at least one new provider, planner-enabled execution, budget-awareness, hitl_tools, hidden tool args, child output_schema validation, tool-result metadata, parallel sub-agents, context compaction, MCP progress streaming, and Temporal execution.
The verification plan must produce artifacts or logs that show which scenarios passed and which were intentionally skipped with reason.
Example: the matrix includes one local run and one Temporal run that both use the same advanced agent config and demonstrate equivalent externally visible behavior.
Negative case: a discovered compatibility gap is either converted into a failing automated test or documented as a blocked scenario with an explicit reason; it is never silently ignored.

stories.title: Gate selected tools behind human approval
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want an agent-level hitl_tools field so that named tools pause for human approval before they execute.
acceptanceCriteria: Add a hitl_tools agent field containing tool names that must request approval before execution, reusing the existing approval primitives or an equivalent shared approval service rather than inventing a separate flow.
Approved requests must execute the original tool with unchanged arguments, while rejected or timed-out requests return deterministic tool-result errors and never execute the tool.
Example: a tool named deploy_service appears in hitl_tools, creates a pending approval, and runs only after approval is granted.
Negative case: a hitl_tools entry that does not match a registered tool is rejected at config time, and a rejected approval never executes the underlying tool.

stories.title: Attach execution metadata to tool results
stories.description: As an Exo user, I want each tool result to include execution metadata so that downstream consumers can inspect timing, approval, and execution status without parsing logs.
acceptanceCriteria: Extend tool-result objects, tool-result events, and related serializers with structured metadata that includes at least status, started_at, completed_at, execution_time_ms, and approval state when applicable.
The metadata must be populated in the core tool execution path and preserved through local streaming, distributed event publishing, Temporal streaming, and web runtime surfaces.
Example: a successful tool call exposes execution_time_ms and completed_at in its result metadata, and an approved HITL tool call also exposes its approval status.
Negative case: consumers that only read content or error continue to work because missing metadata fields use stable defaults rather than breaking existing shapes.
